summary: Joel responds to a positive review of FogBUGZ that criticized its lack of customizability. He explains that this was a deliberate design tradeoff: rather than adding features like per-programmer bug count reports that help close sales, they chose to omit features that enable bad management practices. The theory is that happier users become evangelists who recommend FogBUGZ at new jobs, driving long-term growth. He acknowledges this is partly speculation and that design decisions are hard to prove right.
key_points: FogBUGZ deliberately omits customization features that could enable counterproductive management practices like punishing programmers for high bug counts
When managers misuse bug tracking metrics, programmers resist entering bugs, ultimately subverting the entire system
The tradeoff is between features that close initial sales versus features that make existing users love the product and spread word of mouth
FogBUGZ's strategy of building for enlightened users paid off with 200% sales growth driven by user recommendations at new jobs
money_quotes: It's a tradeoff between implementing features that make the sale, versus implementing features that, we think, will make people who use our software love it, which helps in the long term.
Typical bad managers will use that tool to punish programmers with high bug counts or reward programmers with low bug counts.
Eventually the bug tracking system subverts itself.
The current system, in which we expect FogBUGZ users to have enlightened development processes, makes us miss out on initial sales but it makes our existing customers happier.
We've found that anyone who has been using FogBUGZ and moves on to a new job that doesn't have bug tracking will recommend FogBUGZ at their new job.
Design decisions are hard that way.
key_insight: Deliberately omitting features that enable bad management practices sacrifices short-term sales but builds long-term growth through passionate user evangelism.
